A founder does not need another ritual. They need a handoff small enough to survive Monday.

caption_linkedin:
LinkedIn growth needs a Friday review.

A founder does not need another ritual. They need a handoff small enough to survive Monday.

A trained operator does not need your whole brain. They need the first 10% made plain.

Here is the 10/80/10 version for LinkedIn growth:

First 10%: you record a voice memo with 3 story angles and define the line your VA should bring back to you.

Middle 80%: your VA owns draft sorting, comment lists, lead note capture, and post scheduling inside LinkedIn, Notion, and a content calendar. One board update lands before 4 PM ET.

Final 10%: you review the 4-post approval queue, answer the odd edge case, and tighten the checklist for next week.

Imagine a 18-item backlog. You should not touch all 18. You should touch the 3 that carry judgment, money, or client risk.

The trust signal is the managed layer: screening, onboarding, login management, time tracking, e-timesheets, and one clear review loop.

That is the frame.

Less heroic typing. More work leaving your desk with a name beside it.

We would rather see 1 boring workflow owned clearly than 10 exciting ideas trapped in the founder's inbox.

If this is the workflow slowing your day, message PLAYBOOK and we will map the first handoff.

The Sunday notes are not a badge.

It says 23 unread notes. It does not show the 14 tiny decisions hiding between them, the yellow legal pad you keep reopening, or the approval someone is waiting on before they can finish their work.

The dread usually has names, invoices, forms, and follow-ups attached to it.

A clean queue is usually less glamorous than a big strategy deck. It is also what makes the week breathe.

We see this with founders all the time: the company is not short on effort. It is short on owners for the repeatable work.

The fix is not a dramatic reorg. Pick one recurring chore this week. Record the first 10%. Give a screened, onboarded operator the middle 80%. Review the final 10% on Friday.

Start with one thing.

If that one thing gives you back 45 minutes a day, you just found almost 4 hours a week that were hiding in plain sight.

That is enough room for the sales call, the school pickup, or the quiet hour your brain has been asking for.

caption_linkedin:
Legal intake gets the 10/80/10.

A founder does not need another ritual. They need a handoff small enough to survive Monday.

When the handoff is written, the founder stops being the default search bar.

Here is the 10/80/10 version for legal intake:

First 10%: you record a call rubric and intake script and define the line your VA should bring back to you.

Middle 80%: your VA owns form checks, conflict notes, appointment holds, and document requests inside Clio, Calendly, and a secure intake folder. One board update lands before 4 PM ET.

Final 10%: you review the 5-client priority list, answer the odd edge case, and tighten the checklist for next week.

Imagine a 31-item backlog. You should not touch all 31. You should touch the 3 that carry judgment, money, or client risk.

The trust signal is the managed layer: screening, onboarding, login management, time tracking, e-timesheets, and one clear review loop.

That is the frame.

Less heroic typing. More work leaving your desk with a name beside it.

We would rather see 1 boring workflow owned clearly than 10 exciting ideas trapped in the founder's inbox.
